label: Formula A5.14
section: THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES
nearby_text: To more formally introduce eigenvalues and eigenvectors, suppose, for a square matrix A, that the vector y satisfies the matrix equation for some scalar value, λ. Geometrically, this means that the new vector resulting from transformation of y by A points in the same direction as y (or is exactly reflected about the origin if λ < 0). For such vectors, the only action of the matrix transformation is to scale them by some amount, λ. These vectors thus represent the inherent axes associated with the transformation given by A, and the set of all such vectors, along with their corresponding scalar multipliers, completely describes the geometry of this transformation. Vectors that satisfy Equation A5.6 are referred to as eigenvectors, and their associated scaling factors are eigenvalues, and together they jointly describe the eigenstructure (the intrinsic geometry) of the square matrix, A. If y is an eigenvector, then ay is also an eigenvector, as $ \mathbf{A}(ay) = a(\mathbf{Ay}) = \lambda(ay) $. Note, however, that the associated eigenvalue, λ, remains unchanged. Hence, we typically scale eigenvectors to be of unit length to yield unit or normalized eigenvectors. In particular, if $ y_i $ is any eigenvector associated with the ith eigenvalue, then the associated normalized eigenvector is $ e_i = y_i / \|y_i\| $.

label: Formula A5.15
section: THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES
nearby_text: The eigenvalues of an $n$-dimensional square matrix, $\mathbf{A}$, are solutions of Equation A5.6, which can be written as $(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) \mathbf{y} = 0$. This implies that the determinant of $(\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I})$ must equal zero, which gives rise to the characteristic equation, $|\mathbf{A} - \lambda \mathbf{I}| = 0$, whose solution yields the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$. This equation can be also be expressed using the Laplace expansion, where $ |A| $ denotes the determinant of A and $ S_i $ is the sum of all principal minors (minors including diagonal elements of the original matrix) of order i (minors, which are subsets of the full matrix, were defined in LW Chapter 8). Finding the eigenvalues thus requires solving a polynomial equation of order n, implying that there are exactly n eigenvalues (some of which may be identical, i.e., repeated). In practice, for n > 2 this is accomplished numerically, and most statistical analysis packages offer routines to accomplish this task.

  "nearby_text": "The eigenvalues of an $n$-dimensional square matrix, $\\mathbf{A}$, are solutions of Equation A5.6, which can be written as $(\\mathbf{A} - \\lambda \\mathbf{I}) \\mathbf{y} = 0$. This implies that the determinant of $(\\mathbf{A} - \\lambda \\mathbf{I})$ must equal zero, which gives rise to the characteristic equation, $|\\mathbf{A} - \\lambda \\mathbf{I}| = 0$, whose solution yields the eigenvalues of $\\mathbf{A}$. This equation can be also be expressed using the Laplace expansion, where $ |A| $ denotes the determinant of A and $ S_i $ is the sum of all principal minors (minors including diagonal elements of the original matrix) of order i (minors, which are subsets of the full matrix, were defined in LW Chapter 8). Finding the eigenvalues thus requires solving a polynomial equation of order n, implying that there are exactly n eigenvalues (some of which may be identical, i.e., repeated). In practice, for n > 2 this is accomplished numerically, and most statistical analysis packages offer routines to accomplish this task."
}

The only principal minor having the same order of the matrix is the full matrix itself, which means that $ S_n = |\mathbf{A}| $, the determinant of $ \mathbf{A} $. $ S_1 $ is also related to an important matrix quantity, the trace. label: Formula A5.17
section: THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES
nearby_text: Observe that $ S_1 = \text{tr}(\mathbf{A}) $, as the only principal minors of order one are the diagonal elements themselves, the sum of which equals the trace. Both the trace and determinant can be expressed as functions of the eigenvalues, with Hence $ \mathbf{A} $ is singular ($ |A|=0 $) if, and only if, at least one eigenvalue is zero. As we will see, if $ \mathbf{A} $ is a covariance matrix, then its trace (the sum of its eigenvalues) measures its total amount of variation, as the eigenvalues of a covariance matrix are nonnegative ($ \lambda_i \geq 0 $).

  "nearby_text": "This is called the spectral decomposition of A, and it is derived below in Equation A5.10d. Hence, from Equation A5.3b, we have"

label: Formula A5.20
section: THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES
nearby_text: If we again apply Equation A5.3b, we can express this decomposition as where $ \theta|\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{e}_i $ denotes the angle between the vectors $ \mathbf{x} $ and $ \mathbf{e}_i $. Thus, one can view a matrix as a series of vectors that form the projection space (the eigenvectors), so when a vector is multiplied by this matrix, the resulting vector is the weighted (by the eigenvalues) sum of projections over all of the vectors (the $ \mathbf{e}_i $) that span the space defined by the matrix.

label: Formula A5.25
subsection: 4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as
nearby_text: Geometrically, U is a unity matrix and thus describes a rigid rotation of the original coordinate system to a new coordinate system given by the eigenvectors of $ \mathbf{A} $, while the diagonal elements of $ \mathbf{A} $ give the amount by which vectors of unit length in the original coordinate system are scaled in the transformed system. If we use the decomposition $ \mathbf{A} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{A}_i $, where $ \mathbf{A}_i $ is a diagonal matrix whose elements are all zero, except for $ \lambda_i $, then Equation A5.10a becomes recovering the spectral decomposition (Equation A5.9a). The last step in Equation A5.10d follows because $ e_i^T e_j = 0 $ for $ i \neq j $. Because of this feature, Equation A5.10a is also called the spectral factorization or eigendecomposition of A.

If we use the decomposition $ \\mathbf{A} = \\sum_{i=1}^{n} \\mathbf{A}_i $, where $ \\mathbf{A}_i $ is a diagonal matrix whose elements are all zero, except for $ \\lambda_i $, then Equation A5.10a becomes recovering the spectral decomposition (Equation A5.9a). label: Formula A5.28
subsection: 4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as
nearby_text: Similar logic yields where the square root matrix, $ A^{1/2} $, satisfies $ A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A $, and $ A^{-1/2} $ satisfies $ A^{-1/2}A = AA^{-1/2} = A^{1/2} $, as well as $ A^{-1/2}A^{1/2} = A^{1/2}A^{-1/2} = I $. Because $\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\Lambda^{-1}$, $\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\Lambda^{k}$ are $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\mathbf{A}$, then $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\mathbf{A}$ by $\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.

Because $\\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\\Lambda^{-1}$, $\\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\\Lambda^{k}$ are $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\\mathbf{A}$, then $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\\mathbf{A}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\\mathbf{A}$ by $\\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\\mathbf{A}$."
}

label: Formula A5.29
subsection: 4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as
nearby_text: Similar logic yields where the square root matrix, $ A^{1/2} $, satisfies $ A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A $, and $ A^{-1/2} $ satisfies $ A^{-1/2}A = AA^{-1/2} = A^{1/2} $, as well as $ A^{-1/2}A^{1/2} = A^{1/2}A^{-1/2} = I $. Because $\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\Lambda^{-1}$, $\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\Lambda^{k}$ are $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\mathbf{A}$, then $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\mathbf{A}$ by $\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.

Because $\\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\\Lambda^{-1}$, $\\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\\Lambda^{k}$ are $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\\mathbf{A}$, then $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\\mathbf{A}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\\mathbf{A}$ by $\\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\\mathbf{A}$."
}

label: Formula A5.30
subsection: 4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as
nearby_text: Similar logic yields where the square root matrix, $ A^{1/2} $, satisfies $ A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A $, and $ A^{-1/2} $ satisfies $ A^{-1/2}A = AA^{-1/2} = A^{1/2} $, as well as $ A^{-1/2}A^{1/2} = A^{1/2}A^{-1/2} = I $. Because $\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\Lambda^{-1}$, $\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\Lambda^{k}$ are $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\mathbf{A}$, then $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\mathbf{A}$ by $\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.

Because $\\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\\Lambda^{-1}$, $\\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\\Lambda^{k}$ are $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\\mathbf{A}$, then $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\\mathbf{A}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\\mathbf{A}$ by $\\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\\mathbf{A}$."
}

label: Formula A5.31
subsection: 4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as
nearby_text: Similar logic yields where the square root matrix, $ A^{1/2} $, satisfies $ A^{1/2}A^{1/2} = A $, and $ A^{-1/2} $ satisfies $ A^{-1/2}A = AA^{-1/2} = A^{1/2} $, as well as $ A^{-1/2}A^{1/2} = A^{1/2}A^{-1/2} = I $. Because $\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\Lambda^{-1}$, $\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\Lambda^{k}$ are $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\mathbf{A}$, then $\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\mathbf{A}$ by $\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{A}$.

Because $\\Lambda$ is diagonal, the $i$th diagonal elements of $\\Lambda^{-1}$, $\\Lambda^{1/2}$, $\\Lambda^{-1/2}$, and $\\Lambda^{k}$ are $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, implying that if $\\lambda_{i}$ is an eigenvalue of $\\mathbf{A}$, then $\\lambda_{i}^{-1}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$, $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, and $\\lambda_{i}^{k}$, respectively, are eigenvalues of the matrices $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$. Note that Equations A5.11a–A5.11d further imply that the matrices $\\mathbf{A}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}$, $\\mathbf{A}^{-1/2}$, and $\\mathbf{A}^{k}$ all have the same eigenvectors, namely the columns of $\\mathbf{U}$. Finally, using Equation A5.10a, we see that premultiplying $\\mathbf{A}$ by $\\mathbf{U}^{T}$ and then postmultiplying by $\\mathbf{U}$ gives a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $\\mathbf{A}$."
}

label: Formula A5.34
subsection: 4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as
nearby_text: The maximum and minimum quadratic products occur, respectively, when $ c = e_1 $ and $ c = e_n $, the eigenvectors associated with $ \lambda_1 $ and $ \lambda_n $. This is a useful result for bounding variances. Consider a univariate random variable, $ y = c^T x $, formed by a linear combination of the elements of a random vector, x. Recall from LW Equation 8.19 that the variance of a sum $ y = c^T x $ is $ \sigma^2(y) = c^T V_x c $, where $ V_x $ is the covariance matrix for x. If we apply Equation A5.13a we obtain where $ \lambda_{1} $ is the largest (leading or dominant) eigenvalue and $ \lambda_{n} $ is the smallest eigenvalue of the covariance matrix $ V_{X} $.

  "nearby_text": "The maximum and minimum quadratic products occur, respectively, when $ c = e_1 $ and $ c = e_n $, the eigenvectors associated with $ \\lambda_1 $ and $ \\lambda_n $. This is a useful result for bounding variances. Consider a univariate random variable, $ y = c^T x $, formed by a linear combination of the elements of a random vector, x. Recall from LW Equation 8.19 that the variance of a sum $ y = c^T x $ is $ \\sigma^2(y) = c^T V_x c $, where $ V_x $ is the covariance matrix for x. If we apply Equation A5.13a we obtain where $ \\lambda_{1} $ is the largest (leading or dominant) eigenvalue and $ \\lambda_{n} $ is the smallest eigenvalue of the covariance matrix $ V_{X} $."
}

label: Formula A5.35
subsection: Correlations Can Be Removed by a Matrix Transformation
nearby_text: A powerful use of diagonalization is that it allows one to extract a set of $n$ uncorrelated variables for any $n \times n$ nonsingular covariance matrix, $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$. Consider the transformation where $ \mathbf{U} = (\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \cdots, \mathbf{e}_n) $ contains the normalized eigenvectors of $ \mathbf{V}_x $. Because $ \mathbf{U} $ is an orthonormal matrix, this transformation is a rigid rotation of the axes of the original $ (x_1, \cdots, x_n) $ coordinate system to a new system given by $ (e_1, \cdots, e_n) $. Applying LW Equation 8.21b and Equation A5.12, respectively, the covariance matrix for $ \mathbf{y} $ is where A is a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $ V_{x} $,

label: Formula A5.36
subsection: Correlations Can Be Removed by a Matrix Transformation
nearby_text: A powerful use of diagonalization is that it allows one to extract a set of $n$ uncorrelated variables for any $n \times n$ nonsingular covariance matrix, $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$. Consider the transformation where $ \mathbf{U} = (\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \cdots, \mathbf{e}_n) $ contains the normalized eigenvectors of $ \mathbf{V}_x $. Because $ \mathbf{U} $ is an orthonormal matrix, this transformation is a rigid rotation of the axes of the original $ (x_1, \cdots, x_n) $ coordinate system to a new system given by $ (e_1, \cdots, e_n) $. Applying LW Equation 8.21b and Equation A5.12, respectively, the covariance matrix for $ \mathbf{y} $ is where A is a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $ V_{x} $,

label: Formula A5.37
subsection: Correlations Can Be Removed by a Matrix Transformation
nearby_text: A powerful use of diagonalization is that it allows one to extract a set of $n$ uncorrelated variables for any $n \times n$ nonsingular covariance matrix, $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$. Consider the transformation where $ \mathbf{U} = (\mathbf{e}_1, \mathbf{e}_2, \cdots, \mathbf{e}_n) $ contains the normalized eigenvectors of $ \mathbf{V}_x $. Because $ \mathbf{U} $ is an orthonormal matrix, this transformation is a rigid rotation of the axes of the original $ (x_1, \cdots, x_n) $ coordinate system to a new system given by $ (e_1, \cdots, e_n) $. Applying LW Equation 8.21b and Equation A5.12, respectively, the covariance matrix for $ \mathbf{y} $ is where A is a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $ V_{x} $,

  "nearby_text": "A powerful use of diagonalization is that it allows one to extract a set of $n$ uncorrelated variables for any $n \\times n$ nonsingular covariance matrix, $\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}$. Consider the transformation where $ \\mathbf{U} = (\\mathbf{e}_1, \\mathbf{e}_2, \\cdots, \\mathbf{e}_n) $ contains the normalized eigenvectors of $ \\mathbf{V}_x $. Because $ \\mathbf{U} $ is an orthonormal matrix, this transformation is a rigid rotation of the axes of the original $ (x_1, \\cdots, x_n) $ coordinate system to a new system given by $ (e_1, \\cdots, e_n) $. Applying LW Equation 8.21b and Equation A5.12, respectively, the covariance matrix for $ \\mathbf{y} $ is where A is a diagonal matrix whose elements are the eigenvalues of $ V_{x} $,"

label: Formula A5.38
subsection: Correlations Can Be Removed by a Matrix Transformation
nearby_text: The rigid rotation introduced by U creates a set of n uncorrelated variables, the ith of which is Because the $ e_i $ are of unit length, from Equation A5.3b we have that $ y_i = e_i^T x $ is the length of the projection of $ x $ onto the $ i $th eigenvector of $ V_x $, which implies that the axes of the new coordinate system are given by the orthogonal set of eigenvectors of $ V_x $.

label: Formula A5.41
nearby_text: An extension of the notion of diagonalization is the simultaneous diagonalization of two symmetric matrices, P and G, of the same dimension. There exists a matrix T such that where D is a diagonal matrix, whose elements are the eigenvalues of $ \mathbf{P}^{-1}\mathbf{G} $. Hence, the same transformation simultaneously diagonalizes both P and G. If one has a series of traits with both genetic (G) and phenotypic (P) covariances, they can be transformed to a scale where the new traits (based on linear combinations of the original traits) are genetically and phenotypically uncorrelated, where the elements of D correspond to the heritabilities of these new traits.

  "nearby_text": "An extension of the notion of diagonalization is the simultaneous diagonalization of two symmetric matrices, P and G, of the same dimension. There exists a matrix T such that where D is a diagonal matrix, whose elements are the eigenvalues of $ \\mathbf{P}^{-1}\\mathbf{G} $. Hence, the same transformation simultaneously diagonalizes both P and G. If one has a series of traits with both genetic (G) and phenotypic (P) covariances, they can be transformed to a scale where the new traits (based on linear combinations of the original traits) are genetically and phenotypically uncorrelated, where the elements of D correspond to the heritabilities of these new traits."
}

label: Formula A5.42
nearby_text: The transformation $ \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{U}^T \mathbf{x} $ given by Equation A5.15a applies to any symmetric matrix, and is referred to as its canonical transformation. This simplifies the interpretation of the quadratic form $ \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x} $, as rotation of the original axes to align them with the eigenvectors of $ \mathbf{A} $ removes all cross-product terms ($ x_i x_j $ for $ i \neq j $) on this new coordinate system. Recall (Equation A5.5b) that $ \mathbf{U} $ is a unitary matrix and hence $ \mathbf{U}^T = \mathbf{U}^{-1} $. Thus,

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Proj}(\\mathbf{x}\\mathrm{on}\\mathbf{y})=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}\\mathbf{y}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{||\\mathbf{y}||^{2}}\\mathbf{y}=\\left(\\cos(\\theta)\\frac{||\\mathbf{x}||}{||\\mathbf{y}||}\\right)\\mathbf{y}",
        "label": "Formula A5.5",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 4,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{x}",
          "\\mathbf{y}",
          "\\mathrm{P}",
          "\\mathrm{j}",
          "\\mathrm{n}",
          "\\mathrm{o}",
          "\\mathrm{r}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.6",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{P r o j}(\\mathbf{x}\\mathbf{o n}\\mathbf{y})=(\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y})\\mathbf{y}=(\\cos(\\theta)\\left|\\left|\\mathbf{x}\\right|\\right|)\\mathbf{y}",
        "label": "Formula A5.6",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 5,
        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\mathbf{r}",
          "\\mathbf{x}",
          "\\mathbf{y}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.7",
        "latex": "\\left\\| \\mathbf{Proj}(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{y}) \\right\\|= \\left\\| \\cos(\\theta) \\right\\| \\left\\| \\mathbf{x} \\right\\|\\leq \\left\\| \\mathbf{x} \\right\\|",
        "label": "Formula A5.7",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 6,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{P}",

          "\\mathbf{r}",
          "\\mathbf{x}",
          "\\mathbf{y}",
          "n",
          "o"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.8",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{x}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{y}_{i})",
        "label": "Formula A5.8",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 7,
        "symbols_defined": [

        ]

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.9",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\mathbf{u}_i^T\\mathbf{u}_j=\\begin{cases}1&\\textrm{if}i=j\\\\0&\\textrm{if}i\\neq j\\end{cases}\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.9",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",
        "position": 8,
        "symbols_defined": [

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.10",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{I}",
        "label": "Formula A5.10",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",

        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\mathbf{U}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.11",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}^{-1}",

        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",
        "position": 10,
        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\mathbf{U}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.12",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{y}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{y}_{2}=\\mathbf{x}_{1}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U})\\mathbf{x}_{2}=\\mathbf{x}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}_{2}",
        "label": "Formula A5.12",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",
        "position": 11,
        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\mathbf{y}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.13",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1,\\mathbf{y}_2)=\\frac{\\mathbf{y}_1^T\\mathbf{y}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}_1^T\\mathbf{x}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1,\\mathbf{x}_2)\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.13",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",
        "position": 12,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{y}",
          "\\theta"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.14",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}=\\lambda\\mathbf{y}",

        "position": 13,
        "symbols_defined": [

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.15",
        "latex": "\\left|\\mathbf{A}-\\lambda\\mathbf{I}\\right|=(-\\lambda)^{n}+S_{1}(-\\lambda)^{n-1}+\\cdots+S_{n-1}(-\\lambda)^{1}+S_{n}=0",
        "label": "Formula A5.15",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.16",

        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",

        "symbols_defined": [

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.17",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{tr}(\\mathbf{A})=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}\\qquad and\\qquad|\\mathbf{A}|=\\prod_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}",
        "label": "Formula A5.17",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",

        "symbols_defined": [

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.18",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}",
        "label": "Formula A5.18",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",
        "position": 17,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{A}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.19",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{1}+\\lambda_{2}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{2}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{1})+\\lambda_{2}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{2})+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{n})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula A5.19",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",
        "position": 18,
        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\mathbf{x}"
        ]
      },
      {

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}=||\\mathbf{x}||\\sum_{i=}^{n}\\left[\\lambda_{i}\\cdot\\cos(\\theta|\\mathbf{x},\\mathbf{e}_{i})\\right]\\mathbf{e}_{i}",
        "label": "Formula A5.20",

        "label": "Formula A5.21",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.22",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}^{T}",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.23",
        "latex": "\\boldsymbol{A}=diag(\\lambda_{1},\\lambda_{2},\\cdots,\\lambda_{n})=\\begin{pmatrix}{{{\\lambda_{1}}}}&{{{0}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{0}}} \\\\{{{0}}}&{{{\\lambda_{2}}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{0}}} \\\\{{{\\vdots}}}&{{{\\ddots}}}&{{{\\vdots}}} \\\\{{{0}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{\\lambda_{n}}}}\\end{pmatrix}",
        "label": "Formula A5.23",

        "subsection": "4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as",

        "symbols_defined": [

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.24",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}=\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1},\\mathbf{e}_{2},\\cdots,\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\right)",
        "label": "Formula A5.24",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as",
        "position": 23,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{U}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.25",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\mathbf{U}\\left(\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\right)\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}",
        "label": "Formula A5.25",

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.26",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{-1}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\mathbf{U}^{T}",
        "label": "Formula A5.26",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as",
        "position": 25,
        "symbols_defined": [

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.27",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{-1}\\mathbf{A}=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{I}",
        "label": "Formula A5.27",

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{1/2}\\mathbf{U}^{T}",

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.30",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{k}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{A}^{k}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\quad for any integer k",

        "subsection": "4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.31",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}=\\mathbf{U}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T})\\mathbf{U}=(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U})\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U})=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}",
        "label": "Formula A5.31",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.32",

        "label": "Formula A5.32",

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{y}}=\\mathbf{B}^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\mathbf{B}&=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)^{T}\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}=\\mathbf{I}\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

  "nearby_text": "The transformation $ \\mathbf{y} = \\mathbf{U}^T \\mathbf{x} $ given by Equation A5.15a applies to any symmetric matrix, and is referred to as its canonical transformation. This simplifies the interpretation of the quadratic form $ \\mathbf{x}^T \\mathbf{A} \\mathbf{x} $, as rotation of the original axes to align them with the eigenvectors of $ \\mathbf{A} $ removes all cross-product terms ($ x_i x_j $ for $ i \\neq j $) on this new coordinate system. Recall (Equation A5.5b) that $ \\mathbf{U} $ is a unitary matrix and hence $ \\mathbf{U}^T = \\mathbf{U}^{-1} $. Thus,"
}

label: Formula A5.43
nearby_text: Applying Equations A5.15a and A5.12 transforms a quadratic form to one in which the square matrix is diagonal, which greatly simplifies the resulting quadratic product, as where $ \lambda_i $ and $ e_i $ are the eigenvalues and associated (normalized, i.e., $ \|\mathbf{e}_i\| = 1 $) eigenvectors of A. The new axes defined by the $ e_i $ vectors are the canonical (or principal) axes of A. Because $ y_i^2 \geq 0 $, Equation A5.17a immediately shows the connection between the signs of the eigenvalues of a matrix and whether that matrix is positive definite, negative definite, or indefinite.

  "nearby_text": "Applying Equations A5.15a and A5.12 transforms a quadratic form to one in which the square matrix is diagonal, which greatly simplifies the resulting quadratic product, as where $ \\lambda_i $ and $ e_i $ are the eigenvalues and associated (normalized, i.e., $ \\|\\mathbf{e}_i\\| = 1 $) eigenvectors of A. The new axes defined by the $ e_i $ vectors are the canonical (or principal) axes of A. Because $ y_i^2 \\geq 0 $, Equation A5.17a immediately shows the connection between the signs of the eigenvalues of a matrix and whether that matrix is positive definite, negative definite, or indefinite."
}

label: Formula A5.44
nearby_text: Equations of the form arise fairly frequently in quantitative genetics. For example, they describe surfaces of constant variance ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]]) or constant fitnesses in quadratic fitness regressions (Chapter 30). Solutions to Equation A5.17b describe quadratic surfaces—for two dimensions, these are the familiar conic sections (ellipses, parabolas, or hyperbolas). Equation A5.17a greatly simplifies the interpretation of these surfaces by removing all cross product terms, yielding Because $ (y_i)^2 $ and $ (-y_i)^2 $ have the same value, the canonical axes of A are also the axes of symmetry for the quadratic surface generated by quadratic forms involving A. When all eigenvalues of A are positive (as occurs with nonsingular covariance and other positive-definite matrices), Equation A5.17c describes an ellipsoid whose axes of symmetry are given by the eigenvectors of A. The distance from the origin to the surface along the $ e_i $ axis is $ \lambda_i y_i^2 = c^2 $ or $ y_i = c \lambda_i^{-1/2} $, as can been seen by setting all the $ y_k $ equal to zero except for $ y_i $, which yields

  "nearby_text": "Equations of the form arise fairly frequently in quantitative genetics. For example, they describe surfaces of constant variance ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]]) or constant fitnesses in quadratic fitness regressions (Chapter 30). Solutions to Equation A5.17b describe quadratic surfaces—for two dimensions, these are the familiar conic sections (ellipses, parabolas, or hyperbolas). Equation A5.17a greatly simplifies the interpretation of these surfaces by removing all cross product terms, yielding Because $ (y_i)^2 $ and $ (-y_i)^2 $ have the same value, the canonical axes of A are also the axes of symmetry for the quadratic surface generated by quadratic forms involving A. When all eigenvalues of A are positive (as occurs with nonsingular covariance and other positive-definite matrices), Equation A5.17c describes an ellipsoid whose axes of symmetry are given by the eigenvectors of A. The distance from the origin to the surface along the $ e_i $ axis is $ \\lambda_i y_i^2 = c^2 $ or $ y_i = c \\lambda_i^{-1/2} $, as can been seen by setting all the $ y_k $ equal to zero except for $ y_i $, which yields"

label: Formula A5.45
nearby_text: Equations of the form arise fairly frequently in quantitative genetics. For example, they describe surfaces of constant variance ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]]) or constant fitnesses in quadratic fitness regressions (Chapter 30). Solutions to Equation A5.17b describe quadratic surfaces—for two dimensions, these are the familiar conic sections (ellipses, parabolas, or hyperbolas). Equation A5.17a greatly simplifies the interpretation of these surfaces by removing all cross product terms, yielding Because $ (y_i)^2 $ and $ (-y_i)^2 $ have the same value, the canonical axes of A are also the axes of symmetry for the quadratic surface generated by quadratic forms involving A. When all eigenvalues of A are positive (as occurs with nonsingular covariance and other positive-definite matrices), Equation A5.17c describes an ellipsoid whose axes of symmetry are given by the eigenvectors of A. The distance from the origin to the surface along the $ e_i $ axis is $ \lambda_i y_i^2 = c^2 $ or $ y_i = c \lambda_i^{-1/2} $, as can been seen by setting all the $ y_k $ equal to zero except for $ y_i $, which yields

        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1,\\mathbf{y}_2)=\\frac{\\mathbf{y}_1^T\\mathbf{y}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}_1^T\\mathbf{x}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1,\\mathbf{x}_2)\\end{align*}",

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{1}+\\lambda_{2}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{2}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{1})+\\lambda_{2}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{2})+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{n})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

  "nearby_text": "Equations of the form arise fairly frequently in quantitative genetics. For example, they describe surfaces of constant variance ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]]) or constant fitnesses in quadratic fitness regressions (Chapter 30). Solutions to Equation A5.17b describe quadratic surfaces—for two dimensions, these are the familiar conic sections (ellipses, parabolas, or hyperbolas). Equation A5.17a greatly simplifies the interpretation of these surfaces by removing all cross product terms, yielding Because $ (y_i)^2 $ and $ (-y_i)^2 $ have the same value, the canonical axes of A are also the axes of symmetry for the quadratic surface generated by quadratic forms involving A. When all eigenvalues of A are positive (as occurs with nonsingular covariance and other positive-definite matrices), Equation A5.17c describes an ellipsoid whose axes of symmetry are given by the eigenvectors of A. The distance from the origin to the surface along the $ e_i $ axis is $ \\lambda_i y_i^2 = c^2 $ or $ y_i = c \\lambda_i^{-1/2} $, as can been seen by setting all the $ y_k $ equal to zero except for $ y_i $, which yields"

label: Formula A5.46
nearby_text: Equations of the form arise fairly frequently in quantitative genetics. For example, they describe surfaces of constant variance ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]]) or constant fitnesses in quadratic fitness regressions (Chapter 30). Solutions to Equation A5.17b describe quadratic surfaces—for two dimensions, these are the familiar conic sections (ellipses, parabolas, or hyperbolas). Equation A5.17a greatly simplifies the interpretation of these surfaces by removing all cross product terms, yielding Because $ (y_i)^2 $ and $ (-y_i)^2 $ have the same value, the canonical axes of A are also the axes of symmetry for the quadratic surface generated by quadratic forms involving A. When all eigenvalues of A are positive (as occurs with nonsingular covariance and other positive-definite matrices), Equation A5.17c describes an ellipsoid whose axes of symmetry are given by the eigenvectors of A. The distance from the origin to the surface along the $ e_i $ axis is $ \lambda_i y_i^2 = c^2 $ or $ y_i = c \lambda_i^{-1/2} $, as can been seen by setting all the $ y_k $ equal to zero except for $ y_i $, which yields

  "nearby_text": "Equations of the form arise fairly frequently in quantitative genetics. For example, they describe surfaces of constant variance ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]]) or constant fitnesses in quadratic fitness regressions (Chapter 30). Solutions to Equation A5.17b describe quadratic surfaces—for two dimensions, these are the familiar conic sections (ellipses, parabolas, or hyperbolas). Equation A5.17a greatly simplifies the interpretation of these surfaces by removing all cross product terms, yielding Because $ (y_i)^2 $ and $ (-y_i)^2 $ have the same value, the canonical axes of A are also the axes of symmetry for the quadratic surface generated by quadratic forms involving A. When all eigenvalues of A are positive (as occurs with nonsingular covariance and other positive-definite matrices), Equation A5.17c describes an ellipsoid whose axes of symmetry are given by the eigenvectors of A. The distance from the origin to the surface along the $ e_i $ axis is $ \\lambda_i y_i^2 = c^2 $ or $ y_i = c \\lambda_i^{-1/2} $, as can been seen by setting all the $ y_k $ equal to zero except for $ y_i $, which yields"
}

label: Formula A5.47
nearby_text: Consider a new variable (y) that is a weighted combination $ y = ax_1 + bx_2 = b^T x $ of the original vector (x) of random variables, where $ \mathbf{b}^T = (a, b) $. Its resulting variance is As shown in [[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]], the collection of $a$, $b$ values that result in the same variance ($c^2$) is the ellipse given by $c^2 = b^2$ $V_X$ b. Variables with a large amount of variance require smaller weights to achieve the constant value ($c^2$) than do variables with lower variances. Thus, on a constant-variance surface, minor axes correspond to directions with the most variance, while major axes correspond to the directions with the least variability. This is in contrast to surfaces of equal probability ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.6]]), where major axes correspond to directions with the most variance. The reason for this reversal of roles is that constant-variance surfaces are functions of $\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, whereas constant-probability surfaces are functions of $\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$.

  "nearby_text": "Consider a new variable (y) that is a weighted combination $ y = ax_1 + bx_2 = b^T x $ of the original vector (x) of random variables, where $ \\mathbf{b}^T = (a, b) $. Its resulting variance is As shown in [[SEE_FIGURE:A5.5]], the collection of $a$, $b$ values that result in the same variance ($c^2$) is the ellipse given by $c^2 = b^2$ $V_X$ b. Variables with a large amount of variance require smaller weights to achieve the constant value ($c^2$) than do variables with lower variances. Thus, on a constant-variance surface, minor axes correspond to directions with the most variance, while major axes correspond to the directions with the least variability. This is in contrast to surfaces of equal probability ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.6]]), where major axes correspond to directions with the most variance. The reason for this reversal of roles is that constant-variance surfaces are functions of $\\lambda_{i}^{-1/2}$, whereas constant-probability surfaces are functions of $\\lambda_{i}^{1/2}$."
}

label: Formula A5.48
subsection: Implications for the Multivariate Normal Distribution
nearby_text: Recall the probability density function for the multivariate normal distribution (LW Chapter 8) Because only the quadratic product in the exponential varies with x, surfaces of equal probability for MVN distributed vectors satisfy From the discussion following Equation A5.17c, these surfaces are $n$-dimensional ellipsoids centered at $\mu$ whose axes of symmetry are given by the principal components (the eigenvectors) of the covariance matrix, $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$. The length of the ellipsoid along the $i$th axis is $c\sqrt{\lambda_{i}}$ where $\lambda_{i}$ is the eigenvalue associated with the eigenvector $\mathbf{e}_{i}$ ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.6]]).

      },

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.12",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{y}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{y}_{2}=\\mathbf{x}_{1}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U})\\mathbf{x}_{2}=\\mathbf{x}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}_{2}",
        "label": "Formula A5.12",

        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",
        "position": 11,
        "symbols_defined": [

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.13",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1,\\mathbf{y}_2)=\\frac{\\mathbf{y}_1^T\\mathbf{y}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}_1^T\\mathbf{x}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1,\\mathbf{x}_2)\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.13",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",
        "position": 12,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{y}",
          "\\theta"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.14",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}=\\lambda\\mathbf{y}",

        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.15",
        "latex": "\\left|\\mathbf{A}-\\lambda\\mathbf{I}\\right|=(-\\lambda)^{n}+S_{1}(-\\lambda)^{n-1}+\\cdots+S_{n-1}(-\\lambda)^{1}+S_{n}=0",
        "label": "Formula A5.15",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",

        "label": "Formula A5.17",

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}",
        "label": "Formula A5.18",

        "id": "formula_A5.19",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{1}+\\lambda_{2}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{2}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{1})+\\lambda_{2}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{2})+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{n})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.25",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\mathbf{U}\\left(\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\right)\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}",

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{-1}\\mathbf{A}=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{I}",

        "latex": "\\sigma(y_{i},y_{j})=\\left\\{\\begin{aligned}&\\lambda_{i}&if i=j\\\\ &0&if i\\neq j\\end{aligned}\\right.",

        "id": "formula_A5.40",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{y}}=\\mathbf{B}^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\mathbf{B}&=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)^{T}\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}=\\mathbf{I}\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",
        "position": 41,
        "symbols_defined": [

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.43",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=(\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y})^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}=\\mathbf{y}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U})\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}y_{i}^{2},\\quad with\\quad y_{i}=\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula A5.43",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",

        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",

        "id": "formula_A5.45",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\mathbf{x}^T\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}=\\sum\\limits_{i=1}^n\\lambda_i y_i^2=c^2\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.45",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",
        "position": 44,

        "label": "Formula A5.46",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",

  "nearby_text": "Recall the probability density function for the multivariate normal distribution (LW Chapter 8) Because only the quadratic product in the exponential varies with x, surfaces of equal probability for MVN distributed vectors satisfy From the discussion following Equation A5.17c, these surfaces are $n$-dimensional ellipsoids centered at $\\mu$ whose axes of symmetry are given by the principal components (the eigenvectors) of the covariance matrix, $\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}$. The length of the ellipsoid along the $i$th axis is $c\\sqrt{\\lambda_{i}}$ where $\\lambda_{i}$ is the eigenvalue associated with the eigenvector $\\mathbf{e}_{i}$ ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.6]])."
}

label: Formula A5.49
subsection: Implications for the Multivariate Normal Distribution
nearby_text: Recall the probability density function for the multivariate normal distribution (LW Chapter 8) Because only the quadratic product in the exponential varies with x, surfaces of equal probability for MVN distributed vectors satisfy From the discussion following Equation A5.17c, these surfaces are $n$-dimensional ellipsoids centered at $\mu$ whose axes of symmetry are given by the principal components (the eigenvectors) of the covariance matrix, $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$. The length of the ellipsoid along the $i$th axis is $c\sqrt{\lambda_{i}}$ where $\lambda_{i}$ is the eigenvalue associated with the eigenvector $\mathbf{e}_{i}$ ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.6]]).

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{y}}=\\mathbf{B}^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\mathbf{B}&=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)^{T}\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}=\\mathbf{I}\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.42",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{x}=\\mathbf{x}",

        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.43",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=(\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y})^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}=\\mathbf{y}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U})\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}y_{i}^{2},\\quad with\\quad y_{i}=\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula A5.43",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",

        "label": "Formula A5.44",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",

        "label": "Formula A5.45",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",
        "position": 44,
        "symbols_defined": [

        ]

        "label": "Formula A5.46",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",

        ]

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.47",
        "latex": "\\sigma^{2}(y)=a^{2}\\sigma^{2}(x_{1})+2ab\\sigma(x_{1},x_{2})+b^{2}\\sigma^{2}(x_{2})=b^{T}V_{X}b",
        "label": "Formula A5.47",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",
        "subsection": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",
        "position": 46,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\sigma",
          "y"
        ]

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.48",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\phi(\\mathbf{x})=(2\\pi)^{-n/2}\\left|\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\right|^{-1/2}\\exp\\left[-{1\\over2}(\\mathbf{x}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu})^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}^{-1}\\left(\\mathbf{x}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}\\right)\\right]\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.48",
        "section": "CANONICAL AXES OF QUADRATIC FORMS",
        "subsection": "Implications for the Multivariate Normal Distribution",
        "position": 47,
        "symbols_defined": [

  "nearby_text": "Recall the probability density function for the multivariate normal distribution (LW Chapter 8) Because only the quadratic product in the exponential varies with x, surfaces of equal probability for MVN distributed vectors satisfy From the discussion following Equation A5.17c, these surfaces are $n$-dimensional ellipsoids centered at $\\mu$ whose axes of symmetry are given by the principal components (the eigenvectors) of the covariance matrix, $\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}$. The length of the ellipsoid along the $i$th axis is $c\\sqrt{\\lambda_{i}}$ where $\\lambda_{i}$ is the eigenvalue associated with the eigenvector $\\mathbf{e}_{i}$ ([[SEE_FIGURE:A5.6]])."
}

subsection: Implications for the Multivariate Normal Distribution
nearby_text: Equation A5.18b motivates the Mahalanobis distance which measures the distance of a point from its mean $ \mu $, correcting for its covariance structure, $ V_X $ (Mahalanobis 1938). As we detail shortly, $ D $ provides one metric for tests of multivariate normality.

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.3",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\cos(\\theta)=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^T\\mathbf{y}}{||\\mathbf{x}||~||\\mathbf{y}||}=\\frac{\\mathbf{y}^T\\mathbf{x}}{||\\mathbf{x}||~||\\mathbf{y}||}\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.3",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.4",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\theta=\\cos^{-1}\\left({\\bf{y}^T\\bf{x}\\over||\\bf{x}||~||\\bf{y}||}\\right)\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.4",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 3,
        "symbols_defined": [

      },
      {

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Proj}(\\mathbf{x}\\mathrm{on}\\mathbf{y})=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}\\mathbf{y}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{||\\mathbf{y}||^{2}}\\mathbf{y}=\\left(\\cos(\\theta)\\frac{||\\mathbf{x}||}{||\\mathbf{y}||}\\right)\\mathbf{y}",
        "label": "Formula A5.5",

      {

        "label": "Formula A5.6",

        "label": "Formula A5.7",

      {

        "id": "formula_A5.19",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{1}+\\lambda_{2}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{2}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{1})+\\lambda_{2}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{2})+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{n})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{-1}\\mathbf{A}=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{I}",

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{y}}=\\mathbf{B}^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\mathbf{B}&=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)^{T}\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}=\\mathbf{I}\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

        "latex": "\\mathrm{Proj}(\\mathbf{x}\\mathrm{on}\\mathbf{y})=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}\\mathbf{y}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{||\\mathbf{y}||^{2}}\\mathbf{y}=\\left(\\cos(\\theta)\\frac{||\\mathbf{x}||}{||\\mathbf{y}||}\\right)\\mathbf{y}",
        "label": "Formula A5.5",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",

      {

        "latex": "\\mathbf{P r o j}(\\mathbf{x}\\mathbf{o n}\\mathbf{y})=(\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y})\\mathbf{y}=(\\cos(\\theta)\\left|\\left|\\mathbf{x}\\right|\\right|)\\mathbf{y}",
        "label": "Formula A5.6",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",

        "latex": "\\left\\| \\mathbf{Proj}(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{y}) \\right\\|= \\left\\| \\cos(\\theta) \\right\\| \\left\\| \\mathbf{x} \\right\\|\\leq \\left\\| \\mathbf{x} \\right\\|",
        "label": "Formula A5.7",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.13",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1,\\mathbf{y}_2)=\\frac{\\mathbf{y}_1^T\\mathbf{y}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}_1^T\\mathbf{x}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1,\\mathbf{x}_2)\\end{align*}",

      {

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.19",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{1}+\\lambda_{2}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{2}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{1})+\\lambda_{2}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{2})+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{n})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula A5.19",

        "latex": "\\boldsymbol{A}=diag(\\lambda_{1},\\lambda_{2},\\cdots,\\lambda_{n})=\\begin{pmatrix}{{{\\lambda_{1}}}}&{{{0}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{0}}} \\\\{{{0}}}&{{{\\lambda_{2}}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{0}}} \\\\{{{\\vdots}}}&{{{\\ddots}}}&{{{\\vdots}}} \\\\{{{0}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{\\lambda_{n}}}}\\end{pmatrix}",

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\mathbf{U}\\left(\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\right)\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.27",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{-1}\\mathbf{A}=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{I}",

        "id": "formula_A5.40",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{y}}=\\mathbf{B}^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\mathbf{B}&=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)^{T}\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}=\\mathbf{I}\\\\ \\end{aligned}",

        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=(\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y})^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}=\\mathbf{y}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U})\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}y_{i}^{2},\\quad with\\quad y_{i}=\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\end{aligned}",

        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\phi(\\mathbf{x})=(2\\pi)^{-n/2}\\left|\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\right|^{-1/2}\\exp\\left[-{1\\over2}(\\mathbf{x}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu})^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}^{-1}\\left(\\mathbf{x}-\\boldsymbol{\\mu}\\right)\\right]\\end{align*}",

label: Formula A5.55
subsection: Principal Components of the Variance-Covariance Matrix
nearby_text: In general, suppose we have an $n$-dimensional covariance matrix, $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$. If we order the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$ as $\lambda_{1} \geq \lambda_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_{n}$, then Equation A5.13b gives the maximum variance for any linear combination of the elements of $\mathbf{x}$ ($y = \mathbf{c}_{1}^{T} \mathbf{x}$, subject to the constraint that $|\mathbf{c}_{1}| = 1$), as which occurs when $ \mathbf{c}_1 = \mathbf{e}_1 $ (the normalized eigenvector associated with the leading eigenvalue $ \lambda_1 $). This vector is the first principal component (often abbreviated as $ \mathbf{PC1} $), and accounts for the fraction $ \lambda_1/\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ of the total variation in $ \mathbf{x} $. We can partition the remaining variance in $ \mathbf{x} $ after the removal of PC1 in a similar fashion. For example, the vector $ \mathbf{c}_2 $, that is orthogonal to PC1 ($ \mathbf{c}_2^T \mathbf{c}_1 = 0 $) and maximizes the remaining variance can be shown to be $ \mathbf{e}_2 $, which accounts for a fraction $ \lambda_2/\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ of the total variation in $ \mathbf{x} $ (e.g., Morrison 1976; Johnson and Wichern 1988). By proceeding in this fashion, we can see that the $ i $th PC is given by $ \mathbf{e}_i $, and that the amount of variation it accounts for is Hence $ \sum \lambda_i = \text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ is the total variance of the vector $ \mathbf{x} $, while $ \lambda_i/\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ is the fraction of that total variance explained by the linear combination $ \mathbf{e}_i^T \mathbf{x} $.

        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\vert\\vert\\mathbf{x}-\\mathbf{y}\\vert\\vert^{2}=\\sum\\limits_{i=1}^n(x_i-y_i)^{2}=(\\mathbf{x}-\\mathbf{y})^{T}(\\mathbf{x}-\\mathbf{y})=(\\mathbf{y}-\\mathbf{x})^{T}(\\mathbf{y}-\\mathbf{x})\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.2",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 1,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{x}",
          "\\mathbf{y}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.3",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\cos(\\theta)=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^T\\mathbf{y}}{||\\mathbf{x}||~||\\mathbf{y}||}=\\frac{\\mathbf{y}^T\\mathbf{x}}{||\\mathbf{x}||~||\\mathbf{y}||}\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.3",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 2,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\theta"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.4",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\theta=\\cos^{-1}\\left({\\bf{y}^T\\bf{x}\\over||\\bf{x}||~||\\bf{y}||}\\right)\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.4",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 3,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\theta"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.5",
        "latex": "\\mathrm{Proj}(\\mathbf{x}\\mathrm{on}\\mathbf{y})=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}\\mathbf{y}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y}}{||\\mathbf{y}||^{2}}\\mathbf{y}=\\left(\\cos(\\theta)\\frac{||\\mathbf{x}||}{||\\mathbf{y}||}\\right)\\mathbf{y}",
        "label": "Formula A5.5",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 4,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{x}",
          "\\mathbf{y}",
          "\\mathrm{P}",
          "\\mathrm{j}",
          "\\mathrm{n}",
          "\\mathrm{o}",
          "\\mathrm{r}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.6",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{P r o j}(\\mathbf{x}\\mathbf{o n}\\mathbf{y})=(\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{y})\\mathbf{y}=(\\cos(\\theta)\\left|\\left|\\mathbf{x}\\right|\\right|)\\mathbf{y}",
        "label": "Formula A5.6",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 5,
        "symbols_defined": [

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.7",
        "latex": "\\left\\| \\mathbf{Proj}(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{y}) \\right\\|= \\left\\| \\cos(\\theta) \\right\\| \\left\\| \\mathbf{x} \\right\\|\\leq \\left\\| \\mathbf{x} \\right\\|",
        "label": "Formula A5.7",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",
        "position": 6,
        "symbols_defined": [

          "n",
          "o"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.8",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{x}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{y}_{i})",
        "label": "Formula A5.8",

        "subsection": "Comparing Vectors: Lengths and Angles",

        "label": "Formula A5.9",

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.10",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{I}",

          "\\mathbf{U}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.12",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{y}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{y}_{2}=\\mathbf{x}_{1}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U})\\mathbf{x}_{2}=\\mathbf{x}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}_{2}",
        "label": "Formula A5.12",

        "symbols_defined": [

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.13",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1,\\mathbf{y}_2)=\\frac{\\mathbf{y}_1^T\\mathbf{y}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{y}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\frac{\\mathbf{x}_1^T\\mathbf{x}_2}{\\sqrt{\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mid\\mathbf{x}_2\\mid\\mid}}=\\cos(\\theta\\mid\\mathbf{x}_1,\\mathbf{x}_2)\\end{align*}",
        "label": "Formula A5.13",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Orthonormal Matrices: Rigid Rotations",
        "position": 12,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{y}",
          "\\theta"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.14",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}=\\lambda\\mathbf{y}",

        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",
        "position": 13,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{A}",

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.15",
        "latex": "\\left|\\mathbf{A}-\\lambda\\mathbf{I}\\right|=(-\\lambda)^{n}+S_{1}(-\\lambda)^{n-1}+\\cdots+S_{n-1}(-\\lambda)^{1}+S_{n}=0",
        "label": "Formula A5.15",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.16",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{tr}(\\mathbf{A})=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}A_{ii}",
        "label": "Formula A5.16",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",

        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\mathbf{t}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.17",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{tr}(\\mathbf{A})=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}\\qquad and\\qquad|\\mathbf{A}|=\\prod_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}",
        "label": "Formula A5.17",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",

        "symbols_defined": [

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.18",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}",
        "label": "Formula A5.18",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",
        "position": 17,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{A}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.19",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=\\lambda_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\lambda_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{1}+\\lambda_{2}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{2}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{2}+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{n}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\right)\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\\\&=\\lambda_{1}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{1})+\\lambda_{2}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{2})+\\cdots+\\lambda_{n}Proj(\\mathbf{x}on\\mathbf{e}_{n})\\\\ \\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula A5.19",
        "section": "THE GEOMETRY OF VECTORS AND MATRICES",
        "subsection": "Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors",
        "position": 18,
        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{A}",
          "\\mathbf{x}"
        ]
      },
      {

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}=||\\mathbf{x}||\\sum_{i=}^{n}\\left[\\lambda_{i}\\cdot\\cos(\\theta|\\mathbf{x},\\mathbf{e}_{i})\\right]\\mathbf{e}_{i}",
        "label": "Formula A5.20",

        "label": "Formula A5.21",

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.22",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}^{T}",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.23",
        "latex": "\\boldsymbol{A}=diag(\\lambda_{1},\\lambda_{2},\\cdots,\\lambda_{n})=\\begin{pmatrix}{{{\\lambda_{1}}}}&{{{0}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{0}}} \\\\{{{0}}}&{{{\\lambda_{2}}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{0}}} \\\\{{{\\vdots}}}&{{{\\ddots}}}&{{{\\vdots}}} \\\\{{{0}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{\\cdots}}}&{{{\\lambda_{n}}}}\\end{pmatrix}",

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.24",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}=\\left(\\mathbf{e}_{1},\\mathbf{e}_{2},\\cdots,\\mathbf{e}_{n}\\right)",

        "subsection": "4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as",

        "symbols_defined": [
          "\\mathbf{U}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.25",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}=\\mathbf{U}\\left(\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\right)\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}_{i}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}",
        "label": "Formula A5.25",

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.26",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{-1}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\mathbf{U}^{T}",
        "label": "Formula A5.26",
        "section": "Appendix: Introduction",

        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.27",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{-1}\\mathbf{A}=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}=\\mathbf{I}",
        "label": "Formula A5.27",

      },

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{1/2}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{1/2}\\mathbf{U}^{T}",

        "latex": "\\mathbf{A}^{k}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{A}^{k}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\quad for any integer k",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.31",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}=\\mathbf{U}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T})\\mathbf{U}=(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U})\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U})=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}",
        "label": "Formula A5.31",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.32",
        "latex": "\\lambda_{1}\\left|\\left|\\mathbf{c}\\right|\\right|\\geq\\mathbf{c}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{c}\\geq\\lambda_{n}\\left|\\left|\\mathbf{c}\\right|\\right|",

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.34",
        "latex": "\\lambda_{1}||\\mathbf{c}||^{2}\\geq\\sigma^{2}(y)\\geq\\lambda_{n}||\\mathbf{c}||^{2}",

        "subsection": "4. A symmetric matrix A can be diagonalized as",

      {

        "latex": "\\sigma(y_{i},y_{j})=\\left\\{\\begin{aligned}&\\lambda_{i}&if i=j\\\\ &0&if i\\neq j\\end{aligned}\\right.",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.39",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{B}=\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{A}^{-1/2}",

      {
        "id": "formula_A5.40",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{y}}=\\mathbf{B}^{T}\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}\\mathbf{B}&=\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)^{T}\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\right)\\left(\\mathbf{U}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\right)\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\left(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{U}\\right)\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\\\&=\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}\\boldsymbol{\\Lambda}^{-1/2}=\\mathbf{I}\\\\ \\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula A5.40",

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.42",
        "latex": "\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}=\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{x}=\\mathbf{x}",
        "label": "Formula A5.42",

        "position": 41,
        "symbols_defined": [

          "\\mathbf{y}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.43",
        "latex": "\\begin{aligned}\\mathbf{x}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}&=(\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y})^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U}\\mathbf{y}=\\mathbf{y}^{T}(\\mathbf{U}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{U})\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\mathbf{y}^{T}\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{y}\\\\&=\\sum_{i=1}^{n}\\lambda_{i}y_{i}^{2},\\quad with\\quad y_{i}=\\mathbf{e}_{i}^{T}\\mathbf{x}\\end{aligned}",
        "label": "Formula A5.43",

          "\\mathbf{x}"
        ]
      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.44",
        "latex": "\\begin{align*}\\mathbf{x}^T\\mathbf{A}\\mathbf{x}=\\sum\\limits_{i=1}^n\\sum\\limits_{j=1}^n A_{ij}x_i x_j=c^2\\end{align*}",

          "\\mathbf{x}"

      },
      {
        "id": "formula_A5.45",

        "label": "Formula A5.45",

        "label": "Formula A5.46",

  "nearby_text": "In general, suppose we have an $n$-dimensional covariance matrix, $\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}$. If we order the eigenvalues of $\\mathbf{V}_{\\mathbf{x}}$ as $\\lambda_{1} \\geq \\lambda_{2} \\geq \\cdots \\geq \\lambda_{n}$, then Equation A5.13b gives the maximum variance for any linear combination of the elements of $\\mathbf{x}$ ($y = \\mathbf{c}_{1}^{T} \\mathbf{x}$, subject to the constraint that $|\\mathbf{c}_{1}| = 1$), as which occurs when $ \\mathbf{c}_1 = \\mathbf{e}_1 $ (the normalized eigenvector associated with the leading eigenvalue $ \\lambda_1 $). This vector is the first principal component (often abbreviated as $ \\mathbf{PC1} $), and accounts for the fraction $ \\lambda_1/\\text{tr}(\\mathbf{V}_\\mathbf{x}) $ of the total variation in $ \\mathbf{x} $. We can partition the remaining variance in $ \\mathbf{x} $ after the removal of PC1 in a similar fashion. For example, the vector $ \\mathbf{c}_2 $, that is orthogonal to PC1 ($ \\mathbf{c}_2^T \\mathbf{c}_1 = 0 $) and maximizes the remaining variance can be shown to be $ \\mathbf{e}_2 $, which accounts for a fraction $ \\lambda_2/\\text{tr}(\\mathbf{V}_\\mathbf{x}) $ of the total variation in $ \\mathbf{x} $ (e.g., Morrison 1976; Johnson and Wichern 1988). By proceeding in this fashion, we can see that the $ i $th PC is given by $ \\mathbf{e}_i $, and that the amount of variation it accounts for is Hence $ \\sum \\lambda_i = \\text{tr}(\\mathbf{V}_\\mathbf{x}) $ is the total variance of the vector $ \\mathbf{x} $, while $ \\lambda_i/\\text{tr}(\\mathbf{V}_\\mathbf{x}) $ is the fraction of that total variance explained by the linear combination $ \\mathbf{e}_i^T \\mathbf{x} $."
}

label: Formula A5.56
subsection: Principal Components of the Variance-Covariance Matrix
nearby_text: In general, suppose we have an $n$-dimensional covariance matrix, $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$. If we order the eigenvalues of $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{x}}$ as $\lambda_{1} \geq \lambda_{2} \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_{n}$, then Equation A5.13b gives the maximum variance for any linear combination of the elements of $\mathbf{x}$ ($y = \mathbf{c}_{1}^{T} \mathbf{x}$, subject to the constraint that $|\mathbf{c}_{1}| = 1$), as which occurs when $ \mathbf{c}_1 = \mathbf{e}_1 $ (the normalized eigenvector associated with the leading eigenvalue $ \lambda_1 $). This vector is the first principal component (often abbreviated as $ \mathbf{PC1} $), and accounts for the fraction $ \lambda_1/\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ of the total variation in $ \mathbf{x} $. We can partition the remaining variance in $ \mathbf{x} $ after the removal of PC1 in a similar fashion. For example, the vector $ \mathbf{c}_2 $, that is orthogonal to PC1 ($ \mathbf{c}_2^T \mathbf{c}_1 = 0 $) and maximizes the remaining variance can be shown to be $ \mathbf{e}_2 $, which accounts for a fraction $ \lambda_2/\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ of the total variation in $ \mathbf{x} $ (e.g., Morrison 1976; Johnson and Wichern 1988). By proceeding in this fashion, we can see that the $ i $th PC is given by $ \mathbf{e}_i $, and that the amount of variation it accounts for is Hence $ \sum \lambda_i = \text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ is the total variance of the vector $ \mathbf{x} $, while $ \lambda_i/\text{tr}(\mathbf{V}_\mathbf{x}) $ is the fraction of that total variance explained by the linear combination $ \mathbf{e}_i^T \mathbf{x} $.
